literal: making-present-to-the-eyes, direct realization; from sākṣāt 'directly, before-the-eyes' + kāra 'making'
invariant_definition: Direct realization — the immediate apprehension of *brahman* (or of the Lord) that completes the path. Functionally close to *anubhava* and to *aparokṣa-jñāna*; in school usage *sākṣātkāra* often emphasizes the realization-as-event aspect more than the experience-content aspect. All schools agree that *brahma-sākṣātkāra* is the culmination; they differ on whether it is identity-realization (Advaita) or direct devotional realization of the Lord (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Acintya-Bhedābheda).

per_school.definition: *Brahma-sākṣātkāra* is the realization of *ātman*-*brahman* identity; structurally identical with the rise of *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* and the dissolution of *avidyā*.
*Bhagavat-sākṣātkāra* — direct realization of the Lord. In Vaiṣṇava usage often *darśana* (direct seeing) of the Lord's form. Bestowed by the Lord's grace on the surrendered or devoted soul.

translator_note: 'Realization' captures the essential. 'Direct experience' overlaps with *anubhava*. The literal sense — making-present-to-the-eyes — preserves the seeing-metaphor that runs through Indic epistemology (*darśana*, *dṛṣṭi*, *cakṣus*, *sākṣin*).
